data.title: DFP: Solid Fund But Wrong Time For Entry
data.description:
Overview

Flaherty & Crumrine Dynamic Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:DFP) operates as a closed end fund that aims to generate attractive total returns from its diverse portfolio of preferred investments. Preferred investments tend to be of higher quality and usually provide consistent income. During this period of market uncertainty, a fund like DFP could be an enticing pick to offset volatility. DFP is an interesting investment because a significant portion of its distributions are classified as qualified dividends. The fund has a public inception dating back to 2013, meaning we have over a decade worth of performance history to reference.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that DFP's price has increased by 7.4%, despite the recent decline caused by President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return is nearly 15.2% over the same period. DFP could be a great choice for income investors since the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. Additionally, the fund has been successful at generating a sufficient amount of earnings to support the payouts.

Since the portfolio contains preferred securities, DFP's price has demonstrated sensitivity to the current interest rate environment. This has caused DFP to now trade at a sizeable discount to NAV valuation, which could indicate that this is a good time for entry. However, I remain cautious about the effects of a prolonged period of elevated rates. It doesn't seem like a meaningful amount of interest rate cuts are going to happen anytime soon, so DFP's price may remain suppressed. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's underlying strategy and investment breakdown.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, DFP has net assets of $458M that is spread across approximately 250 holdings. We can see that Banks make up the largest portion of DFP's portfolio at 56.4%. This is followed by an allocation to Insurance and Utility investments, accounting for 18.8% and 11.6% of assets respectively. However, the combination of Bank and Insurance investments makes up over 75% of the fund's assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on DFP's daily price changes and overall total return potential.

The fund expands upon its diversity by also providing some global exposure to markets outside of the US. Therefore, DFP could be a way to slowly start diversifying your income sources outside of US securities. The breakdown is as follows:

  • United States: 67.9%
  • United Kingdom: 8.3%
  • Canada: 8.2%
  • France: 6%
  • Spain: 4.8%
  • Bermuda: 1.4%
  • Mexico: 1.3%
  • Ireland: 0.9%
  • Switzerland: 0.5%
  • Australia: 0.5%
  • Netherlands: 0.2%
  • Germany: 0.1%

The fund utilizes leverage as part of its strategy in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. DFP currently has leverage in the amount of 38.7% of assets, which I find to be pretty aggressive during this period of elevated interest rates. Since higher interest rates directly translate to a higher cost of debt maintenance, DFP needs to ensure that this leverage is yielding positive results. Investors should consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards a poor investment. Ideally, I would like to see leverage come down to at least below 30% while interest rates are high.

When observing the top issuers within DFP, we can see many notable names of some large-cap companies. For instance, Citigroup (C) is the largest individual issuer. This is followed by varying weights to other notable names like Morgan Stanley (MS), MetLife (MET), and Goldman Sachs (GS) just to name a few.

Valuation

Since DFP operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 9.4%. For reference, DFP traded at an average discount to NAV of nearly 7.8% over the last three-year period, indicating that the current discount is attractive. Looking over the last ten years below, we can see that DFP once traded in the premium to NAV territory between 2020 and 2021. Coincidentally, this was the period when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic.

Despite interest rates weighing down the price to NAV valuation, the fund has demonstrated solid fundamentals. For instance, net investment income was $1.26 per share while net realized gains amounted to $2.71 per share for 2024. This resulted in a net increase of $3.97 per share, which left plenty of excess after the distributions were paid out. As a result, DFP has demonstrated sizeable NAV growth since its low of last year, despite these unfavorable conditions. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $26.51
  • 2021: $26.83
  • 2022: $20.86
  • 2023: $19.70
  • 2024: $22.36

Once interest rates start to come down, I believe that DFP will be able to capture a greater amount of NAV growth over time. For now, I do think that resilience should be commended. Looking back at the performance since inception, we can see that DFP's price only took a downturn once interest rates were increased throughout 2022.

Risk Profile

Investors should be aware that there is a portion of DFP's securities are rated below investment grade. For reference, ratings below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade, while anything between a AAA to BBB is classified as investment grade. The breakdown according to the fund's overview on credit quality is as follows:

  • AAA: 0%
  • AA: 0%
  • A: 0.4%
  • BBB: 47.8%
  • BB: 34.9%
  • B: 1.9%
  • Below B: 0.3%
  • Not Rated: 14.7%

A higher interest rate environment means that the cost of debt is elevated. DFP holds a sizeable amount of leverage, which could negatively impact the fund's earnings. Additionally, there's a chance that the lower rated securities within DFP's portfolio could go through a default.

Looking at the graph below, we can see the clear inverse relationship that DFP's price has shared with the federal funds rate. When rates were cut to near zero levels throughout 2020 and 2021, DFP's price rapidly increased. Conversely, DFP's price retracted to the downside when the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates to decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023.

Therefore, future interest rates can serve as a catalyst for price growth in the future. The only variable is that we don't necessarily know how aggressive rate cuts may be or when they'll actually happen. It seems like the Fed is waiting to see the effects of the tariffs that President Trump has put into place before making a decision. However, inflationary data remains higher than anticipated, which could delay the pace at which rates are cut. Depending on which direction inflation goes, there's even a chance that interest rates are increased again, which would drive DFP's price lower.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1174 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we can have an estimated annual payout of $1.4088 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, DFP has had no issues generating earnings in excess of this distribution amount. We can see that the monthly payouts were slightly reduced throughout 2023. However, the current payout is so conservative that I do not believe management has a reason to implement additional cuts, assuming that interest rates do not get cut. Depending on which direction interest rates go, there is a possibility that the payouts can get reduced if the price falls.

One of the most notable aspects of DFP is that 98% of the distributions were classified as qualified dividend income. So despite all of the macroeconomic challenges the fund has to navigate, this is one of the most attractive aspects of the fund because it enables retired investors to collect a high income with little tax consequences.

Qualified dividends have more favorable tax treatment compared to the ordinary dividends you may receive from other high-yield funds. This makes it possible for DFP to be utilized outside of traditional tax advantaged accounts. However, I would consult with a tax professional to determine what the best use case for your specific situation.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate DFP as a hold for now due to the uncertainty around the interest rate environment. We have yet to see the effects of the tariffs and inflation remains higher than anticipated, so I believe there's a chance that the Fed will raise interest rates. This would ultimately drive DFP's price lower, which can present a more attractive opportunity for entry. Additionally, the fund utilizes a large amount of leverage, which could slow the fund's growth in a higher interest rate environment. Despite these challenges, DFP has demonstrated NAV resilience and strong earnings in excess of its distributions. I like the fund's diversity, global exposure, and qualified dividends that it provides. I just don't think that entry here is the most ideal.

Introduction

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. Furthermore, with around 700 companies on the list, the sheer amount of data can quickly become overwhelming. In this weekly series, I highlight recent and upcoming dividend related activity for dividend stocks on the Dividend Champions list.

data.title: 2025 REIT Market Perspectives And A Look Ahead
data.description:
By Kelly Rush, CFA, Chief Executive Officer, Public Real Assets & Chief Investment Officer, Real Estate Securities | Todd Kellenberger, CFA, Client Portfolio Manager, Real Estate Securities | Nina Liu, CFA, Portfolio Specialist, Listed Real Assets

Risk off! Macro and political uncertainty may favor defense in the year ahead

Markets are experiencing incredible turbulence this year with Trump imposed tariffs, sparking a global trade war and increasing geopolitical tensions. With uncertainty on how these issues will be resolved, downside risks to markets are rising and investors should consider diversifying portfolio risk. In this environment, the performance of listed REITs has been resilient, with REITs off to one of the best starts relative to equity markets in a decade. This paper discusses how listed REITs offer a compelling opportunity as a valuable tool for navigating a challenging year ahead.

Key reasons to own REITs include:

  • REITs are likely to be favored by investors in a defensive market rotation
  • REITs are relatively insulated from tariffs and other Trump policies
  • Slower economic growth is likely and so are lower real yields. Falling real yields can be a big tailwind for REIT market performance.
  • Real estate and REIT valuations were already depressed and screening cheap before ‘Liberation Day’
  • Fundamentals are steady with supply slowing and earnings are durable across most sectors
  • REITs provide attractive diversification with low correlations to Mag 7 stocks

Caveats to our outlook:

  • A significant economic recession would likely create a material sell-off for all risk assets, including REITs.
  • Stagflation is a risk, but this time may be different. Post ‘Liberation Day’ long-term market-based inflation expectations remain anchored. If the Fed judges tariffs as transitory inflation, this could create a scenario whereby the Fed cuts rates as it prioritizes the employment part of its mandate in an economy facing a recession.

Reason 1

A notable defensive rotation is underway this year, benefitting REIT stocks

President Trump’s ambitious policy actions aimed at reshaping the U.S. economic landscape and foreign policy have generated greater uncertainty in markets and the economic outlook.

Defensive areas of the equity market are outperforming, including listed REITs, and U.S. markets have lagged non-U.S. markets. For U.S. REITs, this is the best relative start versus the S&P since 2008 and the 4th best since 1999. A similar story is evident within REIT markets as defensive property sectors outperform cyclical sectors and global REIT indices outperform the U.S.

As of 7 April 2025. Source: FactSet. FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs (U.S. REITs), S&P 500 (U.S. equities), FTSE EPRA NAREIT Developed (global REITs), and MSCI World (global equities) Indices. Returns in USD. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance and should not be relied upon to make investment decisions. Index performance information reflects no deduction for fees, expenses, or taxes. Indices are unmanaged and individuals cannot invest directly in an index. Does not represent any investment strategy.

Reason 2

REITs are relatively insulated from tariffs and other Trump policies

Issue

Risks and impact on listed REITs

Tariffs

No direct impact on listed REIT companies. Indirect impacts are uncertain as Trump’s tariff policy can change quickly, but the worst potential outcome is tariff-induced inflation will create U.S. stagflation. Rising yields and slowing growth would be a headwind for real estate unless the Fed can cut rates to support employment. If a moderate economic recession occurs or the U.S. can skirt one, REITs likely outperform equities given tailwinds from easing monetary conditions and defensive market rotation and may even deliver positive absolute returns.

  • Industrial REITs may see the largest indirect effects, particularly assets located in U.S. port markets with significant Chinese exposure. Lower trade volume could reduce tenant demand in these markets.
  • Retail faces risks primarily from potential shortages, though most major operators have taken proactive measures to maintain inventory levels. The greater concern is consumer inflation, which will weight on overall spending levels and retailer health.
  • Data centers rely on critical components such as servers, networking equipment and semiconductors sourced from North Asia which is at the heart of the tariffs. While semiconductors are exempt from tariffs, the other components are not. This could slow cloud and AI investments.
  • Construction costs rising from tariffs on Canadian aluminum, steel, and lumber could pose a significant challenge to new construction.

U.S. REITs derive only a small percentage of earnings from new development and the impact of lower supply across real estate markets would be a positive for REITs.

Foreign Policy

Risks or impacts are likely indirect to REITs. U.S. capital markets and the U.S. dollar could be negatively impacted by any derisking from America that other countries might take. Increases in fiscal stimulus packages or new trade agreements from other countries could be a positive tailwind for non-U.S. REITs.

DOGE

REIT exposure to federal government leases is relatively low. A return to office mandate for federal employees is likely a net positive. The larger risk is to the office and apartment markets where there are job losses and cuts to federal contracts and grants for other industries.

Immigration

Risks or impacts from labor shortages in industries that support real estate (construction, property maintenance, etc.) are still uncertain.

Deregulation & taxes

Currently, we are not aware of any new policies on regulations or taxes that pose a risk to REITs. Any impact is likely to be an indirect positive as markets respond favorably to measures to stimulate economic growth via tax cuts or deregulation. Banking deregulation for instance could help bolster the supply of credit to the REIT sector.

Reason 3

What will drive REIT returns? The answer is often real yields

The recipe that makes REITs a compelling investment today already contains two important ingredients – cheap valuations and positive fundamentals. A third and often the most important ingredient has been missing the past few years – accommodative interest rates. Yields have been volatile from one quarter to the next and moving higher year-after-year. The result has been a generational repricing of listed REITs and real estate since 2022.

Trends have shifted year-to-date, and yields have come back down, along with a growing concerns of slowing growth for the economy. Lower long-term yields are a positive for this longer duration, rate sensitive asset class and real yields importantly drive pricing for property.

Today, the relationship of REIT returns and changes in real yields is at extreme levels, with a -0.9 correlation. That’s a problem when yields are rising, but a decline going forward would be welcomed relief for REIT stocks, as it has been historically.

Current REIT sensitivity to changes in real yields (large inverse correlation) is meaningfully higher than long-term averages

Historically, when real yields have fallen, U.S. REITs have had strong total and excess returns relative to equities

As of 31 March 2025. Source: FactSet. Real yields represented by the US 10-year TIPS yield. Returns data is showing FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs total returns and excess returns over the S&P 500 during the last 7 periods of falling real yields (a sustained decrease of at least 75 bps). The rolling 12-month correlation average is since the earliest available real yields data (31 January 1997). Past performance does not guarantee future results. Indices are unmanaged and do not take into account fees, expenses, and transaction costs and it is not possible to invest in an index.

Reason 4

Listed REITs are offering a compelling entry point for long-term investors

Listed REITs are cheap relative to equities. That’s been true for years now, as superior Mag 7 earnings growth and rising real yields have mattered more than valuation. The depressed valuations of REITs even before the start of tariff induced market volatility may provide some stability to stock prices in the near term. But more importantly, it gives us conviction in today’s entry point as an avenue for achieving attractive, long-term returns versus equities. Principal’s Long-term Capital Market Assumptions report cites, U.S. and global REITs are expected to deliver the highest returns in the next 10 years amongst all equity indices with expensive valuations a drag on broader equity market returns.[1] REIT valuations versus private real estate screen attractive too, with REIT stock prices now trading at a discount to Net Asset Value (NAV) estimates.

Listed global REITs are trading near historically cheap relative valuations to equities

Listed U.S. REITs are trading at discounts to their long-term average Price to Net Asset value spreads

[1] 10-yr Expected Annual Geometric Returns for FTSE NAREIT US REITs is 9.1% and FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index is 8.4% as of 31 December 2024.

Left chart: As of 7 April 2025. Source: FactSet. Enterprise Value (EV) to EBITDA price multiples are used to measure valuations. The spread plotted is the EV/EBITDA of the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed index minus the EV/EBITDA of the MSCI World index. Expensive and cheap valuations are represented by spreads higher or lower than one standard deviation from the mean, respectively. Fair value is represented by valuations between one standard deviation from the mean. The average is since 28 February 2005. Right chart: As of 7 April 2025. Source: FactSet. This chart illustrates the weighted average Price/Net Asset Value of the stocks in MSCI U.S. REIT Index. The average is since 31 December 1992. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future return. Index performance information reflects no deduction for fees, expenses, or taxes. Indices are unmanaged and individuals cannot invest directly in an index. Does not represent any investment strategy.

Reason 5

Company fundamentals are expected to remain steady

With a macro backdrop starting to wobble, it’s important to realize the steady, durable earnings that REITs offer. REIT earnings have some tailwinds building, with new supply starting to come down after construction starts cooled the past two years. We also expect commercial real estate transaction activity to pick up in 2025 provided capital markets hold up. Many REITs, especially those trading at premium to NAVs, enjoy the advantage of both accessible and relatively attractive priced debt and equity capital. We expect this trend to continue in the year ahead, putting listed REITs in a good position to create long-term value for shareholders. This is especially true given 2025 should be a good vintage to buy real estate for the next cycle, in our opinion.

The supply of new real estate buildings has peaked and trending lower, a tailwind for future earnings growth[1]

REIT earnings are less volatile, making it more durable when growth slows

Dividend yields plus expected earnings growth provides some resiliency to future potential returns of REITs

As of 31 December 2024. Source: FactSet, Principal Asset Management. Earnings growth from the FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs, S&P 500, and FTSE EPRA NAREIT Developed indices. Dividend yield reflects values from 31 December 2024.

[1] Source: Principal Real Estate, December 2024. Reflects total completions for multifamily, industrial, office, and retail. This reflects our views on relative opportunities over a 12-month horizon. It is not intended to be, nor should it be relied upon in any way as a forecast or guarantee of future events regarding particular investments or the markets in general.

Reason 6

Diversifying with REITs remains beneficial and is increasingly relevant today

In an environment where equity markets are dominated by a handful of expensive, high-growth stocks (the Mag 7), diversifying sources of risk in an investor's portfolio is more important than ever. REITs provide a compelling alternative with low correlations to these mega-cap tech names, offering a differentiated return profile. Longer duration REITs can also serve as a valuable counterbalance against other stocks with greater economic sensitivity, as REIT earnings are tied to contractual longer-term leases and property fundamentals. Over longer-term investment horizons, REITs behave more like private real estate, reinforcing their role as a portfolio diversifier. As investment horizons lengthen, REIT performance has historically decoupled from public equities, aligning more closely with the fundamental value of the properties they own. This makes REITs an attractive option for investors seeking both liquidity and the stability of real assets in a portfolio.

Defensive REITs offer low correlations to the over-concentrated Mag 7

Long term, REITs behave more like real estate and less like stocks

Left chart: As of 31 March 2025. Source: FactSet. Right chart: As of 31 December 2024. Source: Analysis of data from FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs Index, NCREIF National Property Index, and S&P 500 Index from 1/1/1993-12/31/2024.

Public REIT returns are lagged 4 quarters relative to private real estate returns to adjust for the return lag private equity funds experience due to less frequent measurement compared to public markets.

Frequently asked question: Is it time for an allocation to global or U.S. REITs?

A decade of U.S. exceptionalism and strong USD helped fuel large outperformance of U.S. REITs over global REITs the past five and 10 years. The investment landscape is perhaps shifting with trade wars and ‘America First’ foreign policy. What was once a straightforward U.S.-centric story is now evolving into a more balanced, global opportunity set – and while U.S. exceptionalism likely reigns supreme, Europe and China are increasingly taking a prominent role.

The U.S. REIT market will continue to be beneficiaries of the large and concentrated exposure to non-traditional sectors with durable secular growth – seniors housing, wireless towers, single-family rental, etc. A potential new era of a weakening USD and tariffs won’t directly impact the earnings of U.S. REITs, but a U.S. economic recession is a concern.

However, for U.S.-based investors, a global REIT allocation could help diversify U.S. recession risks and take advantage of a weaker USD. Potential fiscal stimulus packages introduced by Europe and other countries could drive growth and investment in real estate in those local markets. Furthermore, any de-risking of America by foreign investors would drive capital flows into countries outside the U.S. Trade war escalation and economic fallout is a risk.

While the U.S. will always play a prominent role in investor REITs portfolios, the shifting investment landscape may lead to a period of new leadership in REIT markets.

A combination of a rising US dollar, strong U.S. economy, and geopolitical effects have caused U.S. REITs to widely outperform non-U.S. REITs the last 5 years

YTD non-U.S. REIT markets are outperforming by a wide margin relative to the U.S.

As of 7 April 2025. Source: FactSet. FTSE NAREIT All Equity REITs (U.S. REITs), FTSE EPRA NAREIT Developed x-US (non-U.S. REITs), and FTSE EPRA NAREIT Developed (global REITs) Indices. Returns in USD. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future performance and should not be relied upon to make investment decisions. Index performance information reflects no deduction for fees, expenses, or taxes. Indices are unmanaged and individuals cannot invest directly in an index. Does not represent any investment strategy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook, April 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D C

The EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently, an estimate for World C+C output from 2018 to 2026, with forecast values for 2025 to 2026 based on the recent STEO in the chart below. The estimate for World C+C is based on World C+C minus US C+C from the EIA’s International Energy Statistics and World crude minus US Crude as reported in the April STEO to find World minus US condensate output. The trend for the condensate data from Jan 2020 to December 2024 was used to project condensate from Jan 2025 to December 2026 for World minus US and then the forecast for US C+C was added to the World minus US crude forecast along with the condensate forecast to arrive at World C+C.

World C+C increases by 2040 kb/d from 2024 to 2026, an annual increase of 1020 kb/d. The growth rate looks optimistic, especially when the low prices of the STEO forecast are considered. Last month’s estimates were 81701, 82677, and 83843 for 2024, 2025 and 2026, respectively, so the estimates are all higher this month compared to last month. I think the EIA forecast for 2025 and 2026 will be too high; my guess is 82580 kb/d for 2025 and 83040 kb/d for 2026.

There has been a significant reduction in the forecast for future oil prices in 2025 and 2026 compared to last month, a reduction of $6/bo for 2025 and $7/bo for 2027 relative to the March STEO forecast. Also, World demand for petroleum liquids has been reduced this month compared to last month.

Demand for Global liquid fuels estimates fall in 2024, 2025, and 2026 compared to last month’s STEO. By 2026, World liquid fuels consumption is forecast at 104.7 Mb/d down from 105.3 Mb/d last month. World liquid fuels production is also lower at 105.3 Mb/d compared to 105.8 Mb/d forecast last month.

It is not clear why there would be an incentive to build stocks as suggested by the STEO forecast, given the very low prices that are forecast in 2025 and 2026. If I were a producer, I would hold back on production in hopes of higher prices.

Note that the stock build in 2022 was due to high oil prices. Falling prices usually lead to a draw in stocks, like we saw in 2020H2 and all of 2021. The forecast for 2025 and 2026 looks wrong in this regard.

WTI is expected to fall to very low levels in 2026; $57/bo is $8/bo less than last month’s forecast. Growth is very slow in US C+C output in 2026 due to low oil prices. But if the price forecast is right, the growth is probably too high; I would expect a decline.

The tariffs imposed by China in retaliation to the Trump tariffs is expected to lead to fewer propane exports to China which will increase inventories and drive down propane prices in 2025 and 2026. Propane prices are expected to fall from 78 cents per gallon in 2024 to 52 cents per gallon in 2026 at current tariff levels.

Natural gas demand is expected to grow in 2025 and 2026 with much of the demand coming from LNG exports, with new export facilities ramping up more quickly than the STEO anticipated last month. Cooler weather than normal for 2025Q1 leads to higher demand growth from space heating in 2025.

The EIA expects storage levels of natural gas to fall from May 2025 through December 2026 which keeps natural gas prices relatively high over that period.

Higher natural gas prices lead to strong growth in output in 2025 and 2026 at 2.2 and 2.1 BCF/d, respectively.

Net exports of natural gas are forecast to increase from 12.5 BCF/d in 2024 to 18 BCF/d in 2026.

Typically, power plants do annual maintenance in the spring and spring snow melt allows hydroelectric facilities to take up the slack during this period. Last spring, a drought in the northwest US curtailed hydroelectric power, but this is forecast to rebound to normal levels in 2025 and 2026. Obviously, there is uncertainty depending on future rainfall and water demand.

Higher natural gas prices are expected to raise wholesale power prices everywhere except the northwest in 2025 relative to 2024. The northwest had higher power prices than normal last year due to drought and lower hydro output.

Looking at the right panel of the chart above, wind and solar combined output share of electric power output rises by 2% in 2025 and 2026 compared to previous year. Natural gas power share falls by 2% in 2025 and the coal power share falls by 1% in 2026.

The annual change in renewable power output grows in 2025, but is somewhat lower in 2026. Solar output growth is roughly half of the gain in 2025 and 2026. I would expect tariffs will put a dent in this growth, along with Trump’s policies which tend to favor fossil fuel over non-fossil fuel (except nuclear power).

Much of the post-pandemic growth in shale gas output has been from Haynesville and Permian.

The chart above is marketed natural gas production (previous chart was dry shale gas only, NGL not included) for all of the L48 (Alaska excluded). The areas of the US besides the 5 main shale gas areas (other on the chart at the bottom) have seen falling natural gas output since about 2012.

The scenario above is based on models of the Permian and Marcellus along with USGS mean TRR estimates for other shale gas and tight oil plays where I assume about 75% of the TRR mean estimate will be economically recoverable. The estimate may be too optimistic, much depends on future prices and demand for energy. The peak is in early 2029 at about 100 BCF/d. This scenario, if correct, will be a problem for LNG exporters unless US demand for natural gas falls.

The chart above presents the EIA AEO 2025 reference scenario for dry shale gas from 2025 to 2050 along with the EIA estimate for dry shale gas output from 2008 to 2024. Also presented is an alternative scenario more in line with USGS resource estimates for shale gas. The AEO reference scenario has a URR of about 1650 TCF and the alternative scenario has a URR of 935 TCF, somewhat higher than my previous scenario, but probably within the margin of error (900 TCF +/- 50 TCF). The AEO reference case looks like a wish cast where any demand for natural gas is magically met.

Most of the growth in tight oil output since 2020 has come from the Permian Basin.

Over the forecast period, Permian Basin growth slows to the point that it is no longer able to overcome declining output from the rest of the L48 starting in mid-2025. Part of the reason for decreasing output in this forecast is the very low oil prices expected in 2026 (average WTI spot price of $57/bo).

The chart above compares my recent tight oil forecast (starts in 2025) with the EIA’s AEO 2025 tight oil reference scenario from 2024 to 2050. My “alternative” scenario has a URR of 72 Gb and a peak in 2026 of 9.67 Mb/d. The AEO reference scenario has a URR of 127 Gb with a peak in 2027 at 10 Mb/d, again this looks like wishful thinking by the EIA, especially the 2030-2050 period.

The chart above are the wellhead oil prices assumed for the AEO 2025 reference scenario, again based on today’s oil prices. These prices seem far-fetched. Perhaps these are the prices needed to make their forecast happen, but very creative assumptions would be needed beyond these oil prices.

Using the updated tight oil scenario presented previously, this is my updated Oil Shock Model for the World where the extraction rate for conventional oil is assumed to rise gradually from 2025 to 2030 to about the same level as 2019 and remains constant from 2030 to 2040 and then decreases. The average decline rate from 2031 to 2050 is about 2.2% per year; URR is 3000 Gb for this scenario. Peak is in 2027 at 83.3 Mb/d, though there is a plateau from 2026 to 2029 and the peak could occur in any of those years. In any case, the scenario will be proven wrong in time, as the future cannot be predicted.

The chart above defines unconventional oil as tight oil plus extra heavy oil with API Gravity of 10 degrees or less (mostly Canadian Oil sands and Venezuelan Orinoco Belt output). Conventional Oil is all C+C that is not unconventional oil as just defined. The URR for unconventional oil in my scenario is 200 Gb and conventional oil has a URR of 2800 Gb.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/19/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

We remove comments under the following categories:

data.title: Hancock Whitney Benefits From Excellent Capital Positioning
data.description:
Shares of Hancock Whitney (NASDAQ:HWC) have been a strong performer over the past year, adding 14%, but they have not been insulated from the recent market sell-off with shares essentially down 20% from their highs. HWC did report solid Q1 earnings this past week, which has helped to stabilize the stock. I last covered shares in early February, rating them a “buy,” but they have since fallen 15% alongside the decline in the market. With updated financials and a much different macro backdrop, now is a good time to revisit Hancock Whitney to see if the dip should be bought.

In the company’s first quarter, Hancock Whitney earned $1.38, which beat consensus by $0.09 as revenue rose by 4% from last year to $368 million. Results continue to be buoyed by strong credit results and deposit pricing. Meanwhile, expenses appear to be running favorable, largely due to lower incentive compensation assumptions given the weaker macroeconomic backdrop. The company is still focused on expanding within its core Sun Belt markets while diversifying away from Mississippi. Beyond hiring more banks to jumpstart loan growth, it is opening 5 branches in Dallas this year.

Outside of this, it is also pursuing targeted inorganic opportunities. Its previously announced acquisition of Sabal Trust is on track to close in May and will deepen its exposure to the Tampa Bay market with Florida becoming HWC’s largest private wealth market. This will add about $3 billion of AUM and complement a prior business bought from Capital One (COF). While a $0.02 headwind to 2025 results due to integration costs, I expect it to add at least $0.08 by 2027. This is not a game-changing acquisition, but it should be a modest positive.

That said, growth investments are not yet really paying off. It does of course take time for new branches and bankers to ramp up, and this effort comes as the macro environment has taken a turn for the worse. According to management, more banks are looking to grow loans than businesses are looking to borrow. This tracks with commentary from the industry more broadly, so it is not really an HWC execution issue in my view.

We have an extraordinary amount of economic uncertainty. US tariff policy has changed dramatically, and it is unknown if deals will be struck or with which country. With so much uncertainty, many businesses are likely hitting the “pause” button, which will weigh on discretionary cap-ex and M&A. This means less demand for loans, and I do not see a sharp upturn from this pause as imminent or likely.

Given this backdrop, Hancock Whitney’s $23.1 billion in loans was down $200 million sequentially. As noted, it is seeing “softer” demand. While management expects low-single digit growth, weighted to the back half of the year, this is down from prior guidance. Nonetheless, it may still prove aggressive.

I am glad to see that HWC is not “paying” for growth by offering overly generous terms to borrowers, which can create loan loss and margin issues down the line. Indeed, its new loan yield was essentially flat at 7.06% despite a lower average fed funds rate. As existing loans reset to the lower FFE, the portfolio yield was down 18bps to 5.84%. During a period of macro uncertainty, I would rather see a bank sacrifice growth to preserve the quality of its loan book, and HWC is showing that discipline.

Its history of disciplined underwriting is evident in its credit outcome. HWC took $10.5 million of provisions for credit losses, down from $13 million a year ago. Charge-offs were just $10.2 million, down $1.5 million sequentially. Q1 generally does have fewer charge-offs, so I would not expect this to be a sustained improvement.

HWC now has $104 million of nonaccrual loans, up $7 million sequentially and $22 million from last year. Some degradation is to be expected as the economic cycle ages, but this remains just a 45bp nonaccrual ratio. Hancock Whitney is carrying $343 million of reserves, more than 3x nonaccruals—a very conservative level.

Now, its Q1 reserves assumed a 40% baseline economic weighting and 60% slower growth, which was unchanged from Q4. While most of the economic uncertainty picked up subsequent to Q1, I do view this as aggressive. I believe a recession is a close call and expect growth to be between -0.5% and 1.0% this year. I view a 2% baseline as unlikely with slow growth or recession likely. As such, I do expect HWC to downgrade its economic outlook in Q2, which will likely lead to $5-15 million of more provisions.

32% of its loan book is directed to businesses, and I see some scope for weakness here in a downturn. Non-owner CRE is just 14%, and just 2.9% of loans are non-owner occupied office. HWC does have $1.3 billion of multifamily exposure, and valuations in the Sun Belt, especially Florida, have come down. Offsetting this, 98% of loans have some parental or personal guarantee. With its strong reserve position, I believe HWC is prepared for a downturn, though I do expect to see some reserve builds.

Given lackluster loan growth, HWC has allowed its deposit base to decline about $300 million to $29.2 billion. I was encouraged to see a modest increase in noninterest-bearing balances, and it appears we are finding a floor here. Given lackluster loan demand, HWC has let higher-cost time deposit mature, and it has eliminated high-cost brokered deposits, which had been $590 million at the end of 2023.

As you can see, deposit costs have come down alongside the fed funds rate, dropping 15bps in Q1. With monthly rates flattish, I expect only a very modest decline in deposit costs in Q2. Still, HWC’s net interest margin [NIM] expanded by 2bps sequentially to 3.43%, which is also up 11bps from a year ago.

HWC has an $8.2 billion securities portfolio. Its 2.78% yield was up 7bps sequentially. As it continues to reinvest maturities, we should see some NIM improvement if there is anywhere between 0 and 3 rate cuts this year.

The company is also extremely well capitalized. HWC has a 14.5% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio. This was up 0.4% sequentially and nearly 2% from last year. Even if we factor in its $677 million of unrealized losses, CET1 is still an excellent 12.5%. This is a substantial excess capital position, as HWC could comfortably run around 10% given its size and asset mix.

This excess capital is why HWC has been pursuing organic and inorganic growth as it could expand its asset base by 10+% without needing any more capital, which would help to further increase its 14.7% return on tangible equity. It also has been increasing its dividend. Its March dividend was raised from $0.40 to $0.45, giving shares a near-4% yield. The company also did $21 million of buybacks in Q1.

HWC has at least $4 of excess capital a share, which could essentially buyback 8% of the company. That would still leave the company in a strong capital position for some organic growth. Given the macro uncertainty, I expect HWC to retain much of its capital position, but if organic growth proves difficult in this lending environment, a more pronounced buyback or larger bolt-on acquisitions should be considered, as HWC is one of the best capitalized banks in the country.

Alongside Q1 results, HWC updated some guidance metrics. Loans will up low-single digits from mid-single digits. As such, NII will grow 0.5% more slowly. Offsetting this, noninterest income will grow about 5% faster, thanks to more customer activity. I remain cautious on the outlook for loan growth and would not be surprised to see loans flat at year-end.

Back in February, I saw $5.40-$5.60 in earnings this year. I see about $0.20 of headwinds from higher credit provisions, given the worsening macro outlook and about $0.05 of headwinds from slower growth. Offsetting this, there should be about $0.15 of benefits from costs and fee revenue. I also expect $0.00-$0.05 of buyback accretion.

As such, I now am looking for $5.35-$5.55 of earnings, down just 1% from my prior outlook, much more modest than the drop in shares. I previously had viewed 12x as a fair multiple, but the macro outlook is much more uncertain, and equity valuations are broadly lower. I believe we are returning to a 2023 paradigm of regional banks in the 8-11x range, based on their capital positions. I believe HWC should trade toward the top of the range, given its capital posture, which points to fair value of about $55. That points to about 17% upside, including its dividend. As such, I view HWC as attractive and likely to outperform, aided by its capital cushion. I remain a buyer and would use the dip to add shares of this quality bank.

data.title: 5 Reasons Why China's Internet Sector Has Low Exposure To U.S. Tariffs
data.description:
"Liberation Day" tariffs have sent shockwaves through global markets. However, one country on the current Administration’s tariff list is familiar: China.

China is the only country that has been in this situation before, as it was targeted during Trump’s first term. Now, China has a tested playbook for handling US tariffs.

As we examine the implications of these new trade measures, several factors suggest resilience for China's internet companies despite the heightened tensions:

  1. When trade tensions between the US and China began to escalate in 2018, China's strategy was to demonstrate resilience through strong rhetoric. In June 2019, the state-run People's Daily wrote that "China has the strength and patience to withstand the trade war and will fight to the end if the US administration persists."1 A few months later, in January 2020, the US and China signed the Phase 1 trade deal.2 The rhetoric in 2025 is using the same playbook to address recent trade conflicts with the US3
  2. The proportion of China's exports to the US has significantly decreased in recent years as China has successfully diversified its trade relationships to reduce dependency on American consumers.
  3. China's internet sector, represented by the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (NYSEARCA:KWEB), maintains minimal revenue exposure to the US (less than 2%), insulating these companies from direct US trade pressures.4
  4. The current round of tariffs represents a broader trade policy shift, with punitive measures being applied to multiple trading partners rather than targeting China exclusively.
  5. Industrial competitors such as Vietnam, Thailand, and other manufacturing hubs are facing severe tariffs just like China, potentially preserving China's competitive position in US supply chains.

1. China's Tariff Playbook

This isn't China's first encounter with US tariffs. Having weathered multiple rounds of trade tensions since 2018, the country has developed a sophisticated playbook for handling such challenges. Chinese policymakers have demonstrated their ability to implement targeted stimulus measures, currency management strategies, and diplomatic negotiations to mitigate economic impacts.

China's experience from previous tariff cycles has equipped its government and businesses with valuable insights into managing trade disruptions. The country has consistently shown pragmatism in its approach, balancing assertive responses with a willingness to negotiate practical solutions. This experience provides a strong foundation for navigating the current tariff landscape.

2. Declining US Export Dependence

A significant trend working in China's favor is its decreasing reliance on the US export market. The percentage of China's total exports destined for US markets has steadily declined, reflecting a strategic pivot toward multiple trading partners.

Initiatives like the Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership (RCEP) and the Belt and Road Initiative have accelerated this diversification strategy, expanding China's economic integration with Asian, European, and African markets. By reducing its dependence on any single export destination, China has created natural buffers against US-specific trade actions.

The reduced export vulnerability comes at a time when domestic consumption is continuing to play an increasingly important role in China's economic growth model. This structural shift toward internal demand provides additional insulation from external trade pressures.

3. KWEB's Limited US Exposure

The KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF derives less than 2% of its total weighted revenues from US sources, creating a significant buffer against direct tariff impacts.4

This minimal US revenue exposure reflects the domestic focus of China's internet giants. Companies including Tencent (10.85% of KWEB assets as of 3/31/2025), Alibaba (12.16%), JD.com (3.87%), and Meituan (6.66%), have built their business models primarily around serving China's massive consumer market of 1.4 billion people, which is worth $2 trillion annually.5 Their services—spanning E-Commerce, digital payments, cloud computing, gaming, and social media—are predominantly consumed within China's digital ecosystem.

The limited US exposure extends beyond revenues to supply chains as well. Unlike hardware manufacturers that might rely heavily on US components, China's internet companies have developed largely self-sufficient technology stacks. This technological independence further shields them from potential supply chain disruptions that might affect other sectors.

4. Broader Trade Policy Shift

An important contextual factor is that the current tariff regime is not exclusively targeting China. The Trump administration has implemented a broad spectrum of tariffs affecting numerous countries and trading partners. This global approach to trade barriers creates a more level landscape for Chinese exporters relative to their international counterparts. China has numerous regional trading partners with growing consumer economies that it can rely on for its exports. Asia represents 60% of the world’s population and continues to be one of the fastest-growing regions globally.6

The widespread nature of these tariffs means that China is not facing punitive trade measures alone. While Chinese goods face significant tariffs, so do products from many other exporting nations. This universal application of trade barriers somewhat neutralizes the competitive disadvantage that might otherwise disproportionately affect Chinese companies.

Additionally, the global nature of these tariffs has prompted discussions about collective responses and potential trade alliances that could benefit China's negotiating position. As other affected nations seek to address these barriers, China may find opportunities for strategic partnerships in trade negotiations.

5. Potential Market Share Gains

Perhaps most surprisingly, the new tariff regime might actually create opportunities for certain Chinese exporters. Some of China's key industrial competitors are facing similar tariff rates. Vietnam (46%), Thailand (36%), and other Southeast Asian manufacturing hubs are also set to experience high US import tariffs.7

This tariff differential could potentially reverse some of the supply chain diversification that occurred during previous trade tensions. Given the new tariff landscape, manufacturers who had shifted production from China to Southeast Asian alternatives might now reconsider those decisions. This could potentially open doors for Chinese manufacturers to reclaim market share in certain export sectors.

The competitive advantage extends beyond tariff rates. China's manufacturing ecosystem offers scale, efficiency, and infrastructure that other production locations cannot match. China's manufacturing sector might find unexpected opportunities in this new trade environment.

Conclusion

President Trump's tariff strategy appears to be a negotiation tactic straight from his "Art of the Deal" playbook, designed to bring China to the bargaining table from a position of strength. The back-and-forth between the US and China so far shows that both countries are attempting to gain leverage before the negotiations start. Nonetheless, we believe it would benefit both countries to strike a deal, but it is impossible to predict the timeline for a potential breakthrough.

While the new "Liberation Day" tariffs present challenges for global trade, China's internet sector appears well-positioned to weather the storm. The combination of China's experienced approach to trade tensions, decreasing reliance on US exports, and the internet sector's minimal US revenue exposure suggests resilience in the face of these new trade barriers.

The fundamental investment thesis for China’s internet sector remains intact. The long-term growth drivers for China's internet economy—including increasing digital adoption, rising middle-class consumption, and technological innovation—continue to provide a strong foundation for the sector's development.

We believe the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF presents a compelling growth and diversification opportunity for global investors, given recent trade developments. China's Internet sector, as an asset class, is one of the least exposed to the US economy and China’s trade with the US.

Citations:

  1. Data from Bloomberg, "China Would Fight Trade War to the End, State Media Says," 6/22/2019. Retrieved 4/8/2025.
  2. Data from Office of the United States Trade Representative, retrieved 4/8/2025.
  3. Data from "China vows retaliation and a 'fight to the end' after Trump's latest tariff threat," NPR, 4/8/2025.
  4. Data from FactSet as of 3/31/2025.
  5. Data from Bloomberg as of 3/31/2025.
  6. Data from UNFPA Asia and the Pacific. Retrieved 3/31/2025.
  7. Data from The White House as of April 2025.

Definitions:

Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership (RCEP): The Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership (RCEP) is a free trade agreement signed in 2020 between 15 Asia-Pacific nations, including ASEAN members plus China, Japan, South Korea, Australia, and New Zealand, creating the world's largest trading bloc covering approximately 30% of global GDP. RCEP reduces tariffs, establishes common rules for trade, investment, and intellectual property, and aims to strengthen economic integration across the Asia-Pacific region while countering US economic influence.

Belt and Road Initiative (BRI): The Belt and Road Initiative is China's ambitious global infrastructure development strategy launched in 2013 that aims to connect Asia with Africa and Europe via land and maritime networks to improve regional integration, increase trade, and stimulate economic growth. The initiative spans more than 150 countries with projects including railways, highways, ports, energy facilities, and digital infrastructure, representing over $1 trillion in planned investments while extending China's economic and geopolitical influence worldwide.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CyberArk Software: Terrific Company, Terrible Price
data.description:
With stock market volatility at multi-year highs, it's understandable that investors are trying to flee to "safe" corners of the market, high-quality stocks that they think will have little to no impact from the escalation of President Trump's trade war. In doing so, however, we have to be careful against investing in overly valued stocks, since one of the key themes that shook U.S. markets was their extreme overvaluation: both versus its own historical levels as well as markets overseas.

CyberArk (NASDAQ:CYBR), a cybersecurity software company, has been rather fortunate to have lost very little of its value this year. The stock is down ~20% from its peaks in February, but it's roughly flat since January and is still up nearly 40% over the past year. The question for investors now is: does CyberArk deserve this outperformance?

CyberArk, in my view, is a classic "high price for high quality" software company. The best time to invest in these kinds of names is in the early stages of a bull market: which I don't believe us to be in. While I think the company's niche in security as well as its financial performance are excellent, I shy away from paying a mid-teens valuation multiple for this stock: as such, I'm initiating CyberArk with a sell rating.

An identity management platform that's flowering in the age of AI and machine identities

Let's first get a good grounding on what exactly CyberArk does within the rather broad umbrella of cybersecurity. CyberArk focuses on a sub-industry referred to as "identity and access management," often abbreviated to IAM.

The basic tenet of this space is that there are a number of different parties that require access to internal corporate systems: be they employees, vendors, customers, contractors, board members, etcetera. Depending on the person's identity, a security credential needs to be issued to give them access to certain areas, but not others. An IAM platform can be thought of, then, as a digital gatekeeper that hands out customized keys based on your role.

The company notes that many large organizations, including and especially tech titans like Google (GOOG), are exhibiting a trend of issuing shorter-duration certificates in the order of around 90 days, which reduces the risk that security controls will be breached. However, managing shorter-term certificates and frequent reissuances requires a sophisticated identity management tool, which is where CyberArk comes in.

Recently, the biggest new factor driving investment into the identity management space is the rise of AI agents. As shown in the chart below, CyberArk believes that machine-generated identities outnumber actual human identities by 45 to 1:

Managing the growing number of automated agents that are now replacing humans in many rote digital tasks is becoming the bread-and-butter of CyberArk's platform, and a compelling reason to upgrade from existing legacy cybersecurity platforms.

Fierce growth for a Rule of 40 company

Amid the backdrop of aggressive AI demand, CyberArk has also exhibited incredible growth rates. In the company's most recent quarter, CyberArk's revenue grew at a 41% y/y pace to $314 million.

We should caution, however, that a large chunk of this revenue growth is due to CyberArk's acquisition of Venafi, which is a company that focused exclusively on machine identity management. The company paid a whopping $1.54 billion for Venafi in a mix of cash and stock (valued at 9.3x Venafi's ARR), and Venafi contributed $47 million to Q4 revenue. Without Venafi, CyberArk's organic revenue growth would have been only 20% y/y - so when we think about what type of valuation multiple deserves, we should consider the organic growth rate as the acquisition of Venafi will be comped in the second half of next year.

Another highlight for CyberArk: the company has a nearly pure recurring revenue base. The copy added an impressive $243 million of net-new ARR in Q4 (or $77 million excluding the contribution of Venafi), and its current ARR covers roughly 90% of the company's revenue guidance for FY25, giving us confidence in CyberArk's ability to hit its targets even amid tariff-related macro shocks.

CyberArk is also just on the cusp of hitting the "Rule of 40", a popular measure of growth/profitability balance for software stocks that adds up a company's revenue growth on top of its pro forma operating margins. In Q4, CyberArk hit a pro forma operating income of $58.7 million, or a 18.7% operating margin; which stacked on top of the company's 20% organic revenue growth puts CyberArk essentially on the cusp.

Valuation and key takeaways

Now, however, is where we get to the meat of my thesis: to what extent is all this strength already priced into CyberArk's stock, which has risen so sharply over the past year and barely corrected alongside the rest of the software industry?

At current share prices near $325, CyberArk trades at a market cap of $16.12 billion. After we net off the $1.32 billion of cash and $409.2 million of convertible debt on CyberArk's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $15.21 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current year FY25, CyberArk has guided to $1.308-$1.318 billion in revenue, or 31-32% y/y growth. Again, this includes the contribution of Venafi, which will have three quarters without a prior-year compare.

CyberArk's multiples sit at:

  • 11.6x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 50x EV/FY25 FCF
  • 90x FY25 P/E

Needless to say, CyberArk has managed to sustain an incredible valuation premium that is at risk of correction. We note that CyberArk isn't exactly alone in the identity access management arena. One of its most notable competitors, SailPoint (SAIL), recently went public again after a 2022 take-private by Thoma Bravo. Like CyberArk, SailPoint also has organic revenue growth rates in the ~20% range: and yet CyberArk trades at a ~15-20% richer revenue multiple than SailPoint:

We should also note the fundamental macro risks that surround CyberArk. The company may not suffer from any first-order impacts from tariffs, but the decline in consumer spending and business confidence may sharply increase scrutiny on enterprise IT budgets and delay new projects. CyberArk may have a tough time landing new clients and persuading current customers to expand their spend. We note as well that just under half of CyberArk's revenue is international (29% of ARR is in EMEA, 10% in Asia) - increasing the company's exposure to the global macroeconomic slowdown as well.

All in all, while I applaud CyberArk's recent growth trajectory and its burgeoning profitability, it's difficult to justify a ~12x revenue and ~50x FCF multiple when a direct peer is trading cheaper, and when many other growth stocks in the software space are trading at fire-sale levels. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: VNQI: International REITs Offer A 10.55% Return To U.S. Investors
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, the Vanguard Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VNQI) has outperformed its U.S.-focused peer, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a total return of ~6%:

I previously covered VNQI in September 2024 with a Buy rating, arguing the ETF could benefit should low-yield currencies (such as the Japanese yen) start to appreciate relative to the high-yield U.S. dollar. Indeed, weak U.S. dollar performance has been a key pillar of VNQI's outperformance so far in 2025.

In this article, I break down my expectations for returns between dividends, capital gains, and currency appreciation, reaching a conclusion that U.S. investors can reasonably expect to make 10.55% per year by investing in VNQI. I think this is quite attractive for an ETF which is defensive in nature and should see little impact from tariff turmoil. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for VNQI.

ETF Overview

You can access all VNQI details on the Vanguard website here. The Vanguard Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index Fund ETF invests in international real estate stocks with a median market capitalization of $5.2 billion. From a regional perspective, the largest allocation is to the Pacific (47.70% of assets), followed by Europe (24.10%) and Emerging Markets (23.60%):

The largest single country exposure is Japan (23.30% of assets), followed by Australia (11%) and the United Kingdom (7.40%).

The ETF's expense ratio stands at only 0.12%, in line with the VNQ's 0.13% expense ratio, making VNQI a low-cost way for investors to gain exposure to international real estate stocks.

ETF Valuation

When considering international real estate stocks, it is important to remember a key difference international REITs have relative to their U.S. REIT peers. International REITs typically carry their real estate at fair value, determined by independent appraisers (the revaluation is typically done annually or every six months). In contrast, U.S. REITs carry their real estate at acquisition cost less accumulated depreciation.

This key difference is also visible when you see VNQI's P/B ratio of only 0.8x, which is well below what you find at most large U.S. REITs (VNQ has a P/B of 2.4x):

With central banks gradually normalizing monetary policy around the world, I think a reasonable assumption one can make is that cap rates (the net operating income of a real estate asset relative to its fair value) will also stabilize around current levels.

If we price in only inflation-driven growth of 2% per year, we see that buying an asset at 0.8x its fair value should deliver gains of about 2.5% before the use of leverage. Of course, REITs typically use varying amounts of leverage. Assuming 40% leverage (a common hurdle for most REITs), I think investors in VNQI are looking at gains to common equity of about 4.1% annually just from the inflation-driven appreciation of real estate.

VNQI also boasts an attractive dividend yield of 4.85%, although its dividend growth history is understandably mixed, considering the negative effect tight monetary policy has had on real estate stocks in recent years:

As such, I think it is not unreasonable to assume that VNQI can deliver a high single-digit return of ~9% to investors before taking currency exposure into account.

Currency Tailwinds

A key factor that boosted VNQI's performance in 2025 was that the U.S. dollar weakened relative to most currencies, as shown by the slump in the U.S. dollar index (DXY) by ~8% so far this year:

Remember that VNQI's real estate is valued in currencies different from the U.S. dollar, with the cashflows coming from these non-USD currencies as well. As the U.S. dollar weakens, VNQI's cash flows and net asset value in U.S. dollars increase.

In my opinion, a principal factor behind recent dollar weakness has been the unwind of the carry trade strategy (triggered by uncertainty surrounding the outlook for U.S. economic growth). As a reminder, the carry trade strategy involves borrowing in a low-yield currency (such as the Japanese yen) and investing in a high-yield currency (such as the U.S. dollar). This strategy had been quite successful in prior years, considering the higher risk-free yields offered by holding your money in U.S. dollars:

As visible from the snippet above, the appeal of the carry trade strategy is especially strong for Japanese and Swiss investors, and to a lesser extent Eurozone and Canadian investors.

VNQI's allocation to Japan and Switzerland totals 25.9%, while allocation to the Eurozone and Canada amounts to roughly 12.6%. As such, we can say that circa 40% of VNQI's allocation is to countries that should see their currencies continue to appreciate relative to the U.S. dollar (as long as the theory of interest rate parity holds true).

We should note that VNQI's allocation to countries with interest rates similar to the United States is also quite significant. This includes Australia, the United Kingdom, and Hong Kong (which has pegged its currency to the U.S. dollar). Allocation to these countries amounts to roughly 25% of VNQI's assets.

This leaves approximately 35% VNQI exposure to a variety of markets, some of which are low yield (such as Singapore) but also high yield (such as India and South Africa). This group is hard to characterize, but overall, I believe it to be U.S. dollar neutral.

In summary, while the 2025 move in the U.S. dollar index has been quite dramatic, looking ahead, we can reasonably expect circa 40% of VNQI's assets allocated to low-yield jurisdictions to continue to appreciate relative to the U.S. dollar.

I think the takeaway for U.S. investors is the following: should ~40% of VNQI's assets deliver an extra 2% per year just by currency appreciation, this will boost returns by roughly 1.6% annually in USD (taking account of leverage and the 0.8x Price/Book ratio).

The bottom line

U.S. investors buying VNQI today at a 0.8x P/B valuation should reasonably expect to earn a return close to 10% per year, driven by three principal return components:

Component Annual Return
Dividend 4.85%
Capital gain 4.1%
Currency gain 1.6%
Total ~10.55%

Source: Author calculations

I think a return of around 10.55% is relatively attractive considering that it comes with little exposure to tariffs and is generally defensive in nature. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for VNQI.

Risks

All three components outlined above come with their unique set of risks. As mentioned previously, VNQI's dividend history is quite unstable. Hence, there is no certainty that you can receive the 4.85% yield in perpetuity.

The capital gain component of 4.1% also depends on a stabilization in valuation cap rates, which again cannot be guaranteed. I would also note that the 4.1% capital gain results from my assumption that VNQI REITs use 40% leverage. Unfortunately, there is no aggregated disclosure as to the level of leverage employed by VNQI components.

I should also mention that typically low interest rates come with weak GDP growth. As such VNQI's circa 40% allocation to countries with lackluster growth dynamics may ultimately result in weaker real estate price appreciation relative to the stronger GDP growth outlook in the United States over the long term.

Last but not least, the carry trade strategy may once again gain popularity. This would result in the U.S. dollar appreciating, which would drive down VNQI's net asset value (measured in U.S. dollars).

Conclusion

The Vanguard Global ex-U.S. Real Estate Index Fund ETF has outperformed U.S. REIT peers so far in 2025, driven in large part by the weak performance of the U.S. dollar. Considering that roughly 40% of VNQI's assets are allocated to low-yield currencies, this effect should also continue in future years (albeit not by the same extreme magnitude as in 2025).

Aside from currency swings, VNQI trades at a P/B of 0.80x at a time when valuation cap rates are widely expected to stabilize. This should allow investors to achieve attractive capital gains in future years.

My analysis shows that U.S. investors buying VNQI can reasonably expect to make 10.55% per year, which is quite attractive for an investment that is largely immune to tariff risks and exhibits low volatility to the economic cycle. As such, I confirm my Buy rating for VNQI.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management: I Know It Looks Bullish But It's Not A Buy Right Now
data.description:
Performance Assessment

In early 2024, I wrote an article entitled, "Brookfield Asset Management Is In My Top 3 Alt Asset Managers For 2024". This has turned out to be a very successful call as the stock (NYSE:BAM) has outperformed the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO):

And I am happy to share that since publication of that article, BAM has indeed secured the third spot for best performing asset management company:

Thesis

A bit more than a year later, here's my updated view on BAM:

  1. BAM's portfolio is improving in quality
  2. A higher for longer rates environment benefits the credit business
  3. BAM is relatively insulated vs tariffs
  4. Valuations are close to fair value vs peers
  5. Relative technicals point toward resistances amid an overall bullish trend

BAM's portfolio is improving in quality

Multiple metrics show that BAM's portfolio quality is improving. For example, the proportion of its long dated fee bearing capital ("FBC") mix is trending upwards over the last couple of years:

Management expects this trend to continue climbing to 92% over the next five years. And FBCs as a whole are growing very healthily in the high-teens to low 20s YoY growth rates:

As a reminder, fee bearing capital is the engine for revenue and earnings growth for asset management companies as this is what the management fees are charged upon.

BAM's fee-related earnings margins have also been ticking up from ~50% to almost 55% over the last few quarters:

Margin expansion and hence improved profitability is generally a sign of a quality business.

The outlook ahead seems bright as well as management is seeing a constructive environment for new flows that would grow BAM's portfolio:

improving sentiment and increased liquidity in the capital markets is significantly favoring high-quality real assets... So there's no doubt we're feeling very, very good about 2025 fundraising. And you're absolutely

right, we do expect it to be better than 2024.

- CEO James Flatt in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

A higher for longer rates environment benefits the credit business

Over the last few quarters, BAM's credit business has noticeably outgrown the overall portfolio. For example, observe how the Credit fee revenue mix has increased from the mid-20s to the early 30% over the last couple of years:

Credit makes up 45% of the overall FBC mix as well in 2024. And this is expected to increase to 54% over the next five years:

So clearly, the Credit segment is expected to be the largest growth driver ahead. Indeed, the trends in the mix of FBC net inflows also highlight how Credit has consistently been the key driver of growth over the last year as well:

I am confident that these trends will continue, aided by the Fed's higher-for-longer stance on rates as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell indicated that they are more inclined to adopt a wait-and-see approach rather than be in a hurry to cut rates:

For the time being, we are well positioned to wait for greater clarity before considering any adjustments to our policy stance

- Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell in yesterday's interview

This led to a sharp spike (from <60% to 85%) in the implied probabilities for the 425-450bps Fed Funds Rates remaining where they are:

As higher rates mean a better interest yield for credit investments, I expect this to be a tailwind for BAM.

BAM is relatively insulated vs tariffs

Many businesses are being hit by tariffs. But BAM seems to be a good tariff-insulated play. Here's why:

A core part of BAM's strategy is to invest in-line with a deglobalization theme:

And this is being done by allocating capital to domestic businesses, or service-oriented businesses (which are not impacted by the current tariffs war), or businesses that have strong price-indexation to the inflationary effects (making it easy for increased tariff costs to be passed onto the customer):

So I think it's important to recognize our business is very domestically oriented. We own infrastructure and real assets that are integrated into the local communities that we operate in, and we're local in every market that we invest in around the world. And really only a small portion of our business focuses on goods and service that travel across borders…. The second thing we would highlight is we invest in critical infrastructure assets that's the backbone of the global economy. These tend to be assets that have very inelastic demand and are indexed to inflation. So the bulk of our business will be largely insulated from any price increases as a result of tariffs if they are to be implemented.

- CEO James Flatt in the Q4 FY24 earnings call, Author's bolded highlights

Out of the five key businesses, only Private Equity seems to be the most vulnerable to tariff headwinds:

But looking one level deeper within the Private Equity bucket, we see that there is a fair amount of exposure to service-oriented businesses as well, so the impact of tariffs is further reduced and according to my assessment, attributable primarily to industrials exposure:

Valuations are close to fair value vs peers

Relative to other asset management company stocks, BAM is trading at a 12.5% premium on a 1-yr fwd PE basis (28.7x for BAM vs 25.5x for the sector median):

But this premium (light blue line in the chart below) is close to the typical 12.7% premium (green line in the chart below) BAM has commanded over its peers:

Hence, relative to its peers, I believe BAM is trading close to fair value right now.

Relative technicals point toward resistances amid an overall bullish trend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of BAM vs SPX500

BAM vs SPX500 shows the ratio prices bouncing after a false breakout to the downside amid an overall strong uptrend. But I see some resistances nearby via a key supply level. So overall, I think the charts are saying this is not the best area for buys.

Takeaway & Positioning

Fundamentally, I continue to be bullish on Brookfield Asset Management as its portfolio quality is steadily improving via higher margins and a greater proportion of long-dated fee based capital. Management commentary also paints an optimistic outlook ahead for portfolio growth, which I think is especially true for the Credit business as that benefits from the higher-for-longer rates environment we are currently in. I think BAM stock is a nice tariff-insulated play as its portfolio is focused more toward domestic businesses or service-oriented businesses or businesses that are able to pass on higher costs easily to their customers.

That said, a couple of things hold me back from retaining my 'Buy' rating on the stock; BAM seems fairly valued vs peers and the relative technical charts vs the broader stock market show some resistances nearby. I may change my stance to 'Buy' if either of these factors (valuation and technicals) turn more constructive.

Rating: 'Neutral/Hold'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
